did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a system for the recognition of malignant pigmented skin neoplasms using convolutional neural networks (CNNs). Specifically, it evaluated architectures including Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNet, trained on the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset, achieving a classification accuracy of 80.55% with MobileNet after image preprocessing.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a neural network recognition system for malignant pigmented skin neoplasms. It details specific methods for image pre-processing (hair removal, augmentation), describes the implementation of multiple CNN architectures (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet) trained on the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset, and reports original classification accuracy results (e.g., 80.55% with MobileNet). Although published in a Conference Series, it contains original technical results and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: System for neural network recognition of malignant pigmented skin neoplasms with image pre-processing
brief_summary: This study developed a system for recognizing malignant pigmented skin neoplasms using convolutional neural networks with a preliminary image preprocessing stage. The preprocessing involved morphological hair removal and data augmentation to address noise and limited training data. The system was trained and evaluated on 32,454 clinical dermatoscopic images from the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset. Five pre-trained architectures (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNet) were compared to assess classification accuracy. The MobileNet architecture achieved the highest accuracy of 80.55%, representing a 3.80% improvement over the original unprocessed model. The results indicate that the proposed preprocessing significantly enhances diagnostic efficiency compared to visual diagnosis by specialist dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the classification system. The dataset contained 10,515 images of malignant lesions.
The negative class used for binary classification. The dataset contained 21,939 images of benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: malignant vs. benign pigmented skin neoplasms
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a neural network classification system for recognizing malignant pigmented skin neoplasms. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) using convolutional neural networks (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet) trained on the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset. Performance is measured by classification accuracy (e.g., 80.55% with MobileNet).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Melanoma Project
datasets_used.description: An international open archive containing clinical dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin neoplasms. The study selected a subset of 32,454 images from this archive.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for training the convolutional neural network architectures (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet) after image preprocessing (hair removal, resizing, and augmentation).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper describes training on the formed base and assessing accuracy, but does not specify a distinct validation set count or role.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; accuracy results (e.g., 80.55% for MobileNet) are reported for the system, implying the data was used for evaluation, but specific test set counts are not provided.
use.other: The dataset was pre-trained on ImageNet for the initial weights of the CNN architectures, but the ISIC dataset itself was used for the specific task of skin lesion classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy metrics and percentage improvements without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Melanoma Project.notes: The study evaluated five pre-trained architectures (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet) on 32,454 images from the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset (21,939 benign, 10,515 malignant). All models were compared against their original unprocessed versions. The MobileNet architecture achieved the highest accuracy of 80.55% with preprocessing, representing a 3.80% improvement. The improvement range across architectures was 1.07% to 3.80%. Confusion matrices were generated for all models but specific values (TP, TN, FP, FN) were not reported in text.

architecture_used.main_model: Five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures were evaluated: Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNet. MobileNet achieved the highest accuracy.
architecture_used.pretraining: All architectures were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (natural images).
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels for NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNet; 256x256 pixels for Darknet-19.

architecture_details.hardware: PC with Intel Core i5-8500 CPU @ 3.00GHz, 16 GB RAM, Windows 10 64-bit

training_details.dataset: ISIC Melanoma Project (32,454 images: 21,939 benign, 10,515 malignant)
preprocessing: Morphological hair removal (using Euclidean norm L2 metric, K1 element radius r=5, K2 element radius r=3)
Data augmentation via affine transformations (random rotation by 20 degrees, horizontal/vertical displacement in range [-3, 3], scaling, cropping, shearing)

classification_or_output_head.classes: Binary classification (Benign vs. Malignant pigmented skin neoplasms)

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological hair removal: Decomposition of RGB image into color components; identification of hair structures using morphological closure with K1 element (radius r=5) and Euclidean norm (L2) metric; threshold zeroing of pixels; dilation with K2 element (radius r=3) to expand hair boundaries; replacement of hair pixels with neighboring pixels via interpolation using Laplace equation; reverse construction of RGB image.

Image resizing: Scaling and cropping to transform images into a single size required by specific neural network architectures (224x224 pixels for NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, MobileNet; 256x256 pixels for Darknet-19).
Data augmentation via affine transformations: Random rotation by 20 degrees; horizontal and vertical displacement in range [-3, 3]; scaling; cropping; shearing.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to increase the variety of training data and balance the number of images in different categories. Specific transformations included: random rotation by 20 degrees; horizontal and vertical displacement in the range [-3, 3]; scaling; cropping; and shearing. These were applied to dermatoscopic images from the ISIC Melanoma Project dataset.

conclusions: The proposed system for the recognition of malignant pigmented skin neoplasms achieved a classification accuracy of 80.55% using the MobileNet architecture with preliminary image preprocessing.

The application of preliminary image preprocessing (morphological hair removal and data augmentation) increased recognition accuracy by 1.07% to 3.80% across the tested neural network architectures compared to their original unprocessed versions.
The MobileNet architecture demonstrated the highest improvement in recognition accuracy (3.80%) among the tested models (Darknet-19, NASNetMobile, GoogLeNet, EfficientNet, and MobileNet).
The modified GoogLeNet architecture showed the smallest increase in recognition accuracy (1.07%) due to the preprocessing stage.
The recognition accuracy achieved by the proposed system is superior to the average accuracy of 65% to 75% typically achieved by specialist dermatologists using visual diagnosis with dermatoscopy.
The use of the proposed recognition system has the potential to increase the efficiency and quality of diagnosis for malignant skin lesions compared to visual diagnostic methods.
Future research directions include constructing more complex classification systems that incorporate patient metadata (age, gender, race, genetic predisposition) alongside image analysis.
important_remarks: The study is published in a conference series (Journal of Physics: Conference Series), which may indicate lower peer-review rigor compared to high-impact medical journals.

The paper does not report a separate validation or testing split; accuracy results are derived from the training process on the ISIC dataset without explicit mention of held-out test data, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported accuracy improvements (1.07% to 3.80%), making it unclear if the gains are statistically significant.

The comparison to dermatologist performance relies on cited literature (65-75% accuracy) rather than a direct head-to-head clinical trial, limiting the validity of the claim that the system is superior to specialists.

The dataset (ISIC Melanoma Project) is known to have class imbalance (21,939 benign vs. 10,515 malignant); while augmentation was used, the paper does not report metrics like sensitivity, specificity, or AUC, which are critical for diagnostic systems.
The preprocessing method (morphological hair removal) is heuristic and empirical; its effectiveness may vary across different image qualities or skin types not represented in the dataset.
The system was implemented using MatLab R2020b on a standard PC (Intel Core i5, 16GB RAM), which may limit scalability or real-time deployment capabilities compared to GPU-accelerated systems.

The study focuses only on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address sub-classification of malignant lesions (e.g., melanoma subtypes), limiting clinical utility for detailed diagnosis.
